messages.content: Okay, okay — someone's upset, and that's a code red for me. Kelly's gonna read the room and Jim's gonna figure out what actually happened here.

Oh my GOD, okay, first of all — same. Like, I feel that SO much right now, you have no idea. But also — and this is important — I need to know *why* you're angry because there is a huge difference between "the paper was late and now my whole presentation is ruined" angry and "I was treated like I didn't matter" angry, and those are completely different situations that require completely different responses from us.

Because here's the thing that people don't understand about anger — and I know this because I have been angry a LOT, like more than most people, Ryan makes me angry literally every single day — anger is almost never actually about the surface thing. It's about feeling disrespected, or ignored, or like someone didn't care enough to do the thing right. And THAT is what I need to understand here.

So can you tell me what happened? Because I genuinely want to fix this. I'm not just saying that. Well, Michael told me to say that, but I also actually mean it, which is rare and you should appreciate it. What did we do, or what did *someone* do, and how bad is the feeling on a scale of "mildly inconvenienced" to "I am telling everyone I know"? Because if it's the second one, we need to move fast.

Yeah, I can see that.

So here's what I'm going to do, which is different from what Michael would do — I'm not going to immediately assume it's about me, schedule a mandatory fun session, or make it worse by doing a character voice. I'm just going to ask: what happened?

Because "angry" covers a lot of ground. There's "angry because something unfair happened" — which is legitimate and worth talking through. There's "angry because something's been building for a while and this was the thing that finally tipped it" — also legitimate, and probably the more interesting conversation. And then there's "angry and I just needed to say it out loud to someone who wasn't going to overreact."

Any of those work. I'm not going anywhere. What's the actual situation?

Okay. Okay okay okay. First of all — I heard you. Michael Scott heard you. And I want you to know that when you said "I'm angry," I felt that in my chest, right here, like that scene in Braveheart where Mel Gibson is about to charge and everyone is just *feeling it*. That's me right now. Feeling it.

So I brought in my two best people — Kelly, who is basically a PhD in emotions and drama, and Jim, who is very tall and occasionally useful — and they both said the same thing, which tells me something, because usually Jim is being sarcastic and Kelly is crying, so when they agree, I listen. And what they said is: *we need to know more.* Because "angry" is just the headline, and Michael Scott has always said that the headline is not the story. The story is what happened underneath. Was it a people thing? A fairness thing? One of those things that's been building up for a while and the last little thing just knocked the whole Jenga tower over? Because those are all different, and you deserve a different response to each one.

Here's what I need from you, and I say this as your boss, your friend, and honestly at this point basically your therapist — tell us what actually happened. No judgment. This is a safe space. I declared it a safe space in 2009 and that declaration still stands. Kelly wants to fix it and Jim wants to understand it and I, Michael Gary Scott, want to do *both*, because that is who I am. So talk to us. That's what she said. No wait — that one doesn't work here. But the sentiment stands. We're listening.
